summary: DHH describes his first day at Copenhagen Business School, framing the experience of meeting two hundred fellow students as a ruthless screening process. He has ten days before students are split into smaller study teams, so he's aggressively filtering for people he finds interesting. He openly dismisses 'nonentities' and justifies the harsh approach by valuing his time. He acknowledges this attitude likely repels many potential connections but sees that as a feature, not a bug.
key_points: Frames meeting classmates as a deliberate screening mission rather than an open social experience
Explicitly dismisses people he doesn't find interesting as 'nonentities' not worth his time
Believes his own high self-regard and demanding attitude will naturally filter out incompatible people
Sees mutual repulsion as efficient — fewer candidates means easier selection
money_quotes: Two hundred fellow students and ten introduction days to sort them through in order to find a few, select potential study buddies.
This has already cast me into a rigorous screening process with little patience for the nonentities.
I value my time, and I honestly can't be bothered with wasting on it on people I don't find interesting.
I think I have something to offer, so I expect a lot in return as well.
That attitude probably already dramaticly cut down on the number of people who're even interested in sharing a learning experience with me - and that's OK. It'll only make my task easier.
key_insight: Even as a university freshman, DHH displayed the same uncompromising, efficiency-obsessed filtering mentality that would later define his approach to hiring, business, and software.
